runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 3 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Congressional Budget Office (2012) How the Supply of Labor Responds to Changes in the After-Tax Wage
McClelland and Mok (2012) A Review of Recent Research on Labor Supply Elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners typically exhibit lower labor supply elasticities compared to secondary earners. For individuals in lower-to-middle earnings deciles (like decile 3), microsimulation models like those used by the CBO typically assign substitution elasticities around 0.15 to 0.25. This reflects a moderate responsiveness to changes in the effective marginal wage.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 3 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Congressional Budget Office (2012). How the Supply of Labor Responds to Changes in Taxes.
McClelland and Mok (2012). A Review of Recent Research on Labor Supply Elasticities.
runs.reasoningSummary: In U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation models (like those used by the CBO), primary earners are generally assigned lower substitution elasticities than secondary earners. Elasticities tend to be slightly higher at the lower end of the earnings distribution compared to the top. For decile 3, a substitution elasticity of around 0.20 to 0.25 is standard in these modeling frameworks, reflecting modest but measurable responsiveness to marginal after-tax wage changes.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally exhibit low substitution elasticities compared to secondary earners. In microsimulation models like those used by the CBO, primary earners in the lower to middle deciles (such as decile 3) are typically assigned substitution elasticities in the range of 0.1 to 0.3. A widely cited consensus value for primary earners is around 0.2.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 3 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: McClelland and Mok (2012) - A Review of Recent Research on Labor Supply Elasticities
Congressional Budget Office (CBO) labor supply assumptions
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally exhibit low responsiveness to marginal wage changes compared to secondary earners. In many standard U.S. microsimulation models (such as CBO), primary earner substitution elasticities are typically assumed to be around 0.10 to 0.25. While lower-income deciles (like decile 3) might have slightly higher elasticities due to greater extensive-margin responsiveness, the overall substitution elasticity remains quite modest. I center my estimate at 0.15, with a 90% confidence interval ranging from 0.05 to 0.40.
